summary: Living in Canada, I experienced the healthcare system that is socialized and funded through taxes, which means it's available to everyone more or less equally. Throughout the pandemic, I worked for the federal government here, specifically in Ottawa, and saw firsthand how the government responded to the crisis. I also traveled between Ottawa and Calgary during the pandemic to be with family, which gave me a sense of the different places within Canada. Overall, Canada’s healthcare and government systems shaped much of my pandemic experience.

I worked for the Department of Health in Canada, specifically in a directorate focused on regulatory review reform before the pandemic. When COVID-19 hit, the department reorganized and shifted focus to vaccines, which made my job much busier. As an executive assistant to a director there, I handled a lot of ministerial correspondence and internal government processes. The department was central to the pandemic response, and I saw how the workload and priorities changed dramatically during that time.

I first heard about the COVID-19 pandemic at Health Canada in early 2020, when it was just called 'the virus.' My workplace quickly shifted as the pandemic unfolded, moving us to remote work and changing our roles significantly. The volume of ministerial correspondence exploded as people sought answers, and my workload increased as I took on additional responsibilities. Personally, I found some positives like no commuting and more time at home, but the overall experience was marked by uncertainty, increased work stress, and adapting to new ways of working.

Ottawa is where I live and work, commuting daily before the pandemic despite the public transit challenges. It’s also where the federal government offices are, including the Department of Health where I am employed. During the pandemic, I worked remotely from Ottawa but also traveled back and forth to Calgary to be with family. Ottawa shaped much of my professional life and was the hub of my pandemic work experience.

During the pandemic, I spent time in Calgary visiting my eldest child and their partner who had moved there to live with my sister. Calgary became a temporary home for me as I worked remotely from there, adjusting to the different time zone but maintaining my regular hours. My sister Aruna, a professor who owns a beautiful house in Calgary, was a great support during this time. The experience gave me a chance to decorate and settle into a different environment while staying connected to my family.

I remember the H1N1 pandemic because it was when I first noticed how ministerial correspondence would spike during a health crisis. The volume of questions and concerns from the public and government officials during H1N1 was intense, and I saw a similar pattern repeat with COVID-19 but on a much larger scale. That experience helped me understand the pressures and challenges we faced during the COVID-19 pandemic in managing information and communication.
